Yes I was referring to the calculation of the withdrawals here:

```
`state` is the state object after applying `process_slots(state, slot)`
     transition to the resulting state of the parent block processing
```

`apply_parent_execution_payload` should be  pretty cheap as it doesn't change validators or balances

my default idea on that is that we will send FCU with attribute at payload arrival (or at PTC deadline) assuming we build on top of full, then at slot transition we recompute the head and eventually change our mind on the fly. 
And this could be a payload reorg or even a beacon block reorg.

This is also how it works today.

author: potuz
content: The head state is the current beacon state at the tip of your canonical chain, and we always used that state advanced to the current slot to prepare a proposal. Now this is not enough

author: terencechain
content:
We copy that today regardless. My point is we dont need the second copy.
With deferred  processing, the spec as written makes it seem like you might need a second copy, but I think that can be avoided in the client depending on how it is implemented, since you do not need to save the post-state at the end.

1. Get the pre-state as a copy
2. Verify the payload in consensus
3. Verify the payload in execution
4. Check if you are proposer for the next slot. If yes:
   4a. apply execution requests, get withdrawals, call FCU

In the spec, there is a copy between 4 and 4a. All I am saying is we may not need that copy

author: potuz
content: Well the problem is that you haven't finished here:  you still need to propose a block so either you keep that processed state in 4a around cached or you will need another copy in the next slot when you construct the block. That's all I'm saying: you need two copies and two processing of the execution requests or a damned complicated cache

author: terencechain
content: im missing something, why do i need this again next slot (in happy case) if im proposing? which part is required

author: potuz
content: You need to construct the beacon block and produce a state root

author: potuz
content: So when do you start executing the stf?

author: potuz
content: Pre or post intermediate state?

author: terencechain
content:
but how is that different than today? you get the pre state, that's the post CL state (happy case) and get the execution requests from DB
im trying to undertand what i need to do that's extra here

author: potuz
content:
Today: at N+1 you get a state, you apply your block on top of it and then you compute state root. 

Tomorrow: you get a state, you apply execution requests from the parent payload (which you had to apply anyway before to compute attributes) and then you apply your block to compute state root. 

If you want to save yourself from twice computing the requests processing you need to cache the attributes computation

author: terencechain
content:
oh i see, you actually thinking to avoid applying the second time and just use the result of n-1
but applying payload is very fast, im not even sure its worth the trouble. More bugs come from caching. I can def benchmark it though
in my current implementation, I do apply the second time with the intuition that pure state transition is very fast. Coping the state is what i actively try to avoid
